text.title: API Error Handling Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every api-error-handling artefact must obey. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every error response MUST conform to RFC 7807 Problem Details: required fields type (URI), title (string), status (int). Free-form error bodies are forbidden.

rationale.source: RFC 7807 Problem Details for HTTP APIs

rule.rationale: Standardised envelope lets programmatic clients parse errors without per-endpoint customisation; one schema replaces N.

rule.statement: type URI MUST be stable across releases (e.g. `https://api.example.com/errors/insufficient-funds`). Title MAY change for i18n; type MUST NOT.
rule.rationale: Clients key off type for routing; type churn breaks every consumer at once.

rule.statement: Every error response MUST include `traceId` (or `trace_id`) as a top-level extension field, populated from the active span.
rule.rationale: Without traceId, support cannot find the user's request in logs; debugging time multiplies.

rule.statement: 4xx = caller-fixable (auth/input/permission); 5xx = our-fault (deps down / bug / timeout). Mis-classification (e.g. 500 for a validation error) is forbidden.

rationale.source: RFC 9110 HTTP Semantics + Internal — error-rate audit

rule.rationale: Mis-classification corrupts alerting (5xx pages oncall for caller errors); clients also retry 5xx but not 4xx.

rule.statement: Error responses MUST NOT contain PII or secret material in any field. Stack traces, raw SQL, internal IPs, and tokens are forbidden.

rationale.source: OWASP API Security Top 10 — Improper Inventory Mgmt + GDPR

rule.rationale: Error responses are the easiest exfil channel; leaks land in logs, screenshots, and customer-support tickets.
